title: SEGMENT 3 — E. Donnall Thomas and the long road to the first successful transplants

lines.text: SEGMENT 3 — E. Donnall Thomas and the long road to the first successful transplants
The history of HSCT is the story of one person, more than any other, refusing to give up on an idea that looked completely insane for the better part of twenty years.
That person is E.
Donnall Thomas — usually just "Don Thomas" — who won the Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine in 1990 for developing bone marrow transplantation.
He shared the prize with Joseph Murray, who pioneered kidney transplantation.
The Nobel committee, in essence, gave that prize to the founders of transplant medicine as a whole.
Let me set the scene.

In the years after the atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki in 1945, doctors and biologists began to understand that ionizing radiation, in sufficient doses, kills people primarily by destroying the bone marrow.
Radiation damages all rapidly dividing tissues, but the most acutely lethal effect at moderate doses is the wipeout of the blood-forming cells.

People exposed to several grays of whole-body radiation die within a few weeks, not because their hearts stop or their brains fail, but because their bone marrow stops making white cells and platelets — they bleed out, or they die of infection.
This was the bone marrow syndrome of acute radiation sickness.

In the late 1940s and early 1950s, a series of mouse experiments showed something remarkable: if you irradiated a mouse to a normally lethal dose, but then injected it with bone marrow cells from a healthy mouse, the recipient mouse lived.
The bone marrow cells homed, engrafted, and rebuilt the blood.
This was the proof of principle, in mice, that bone marrow could be transplanted between individuals.
The leap to humans was made — slowly, painfully — by a small number of clinicians in the late 1950s.

Thomas, then a young hematologist at Cooperstown, New York, did the very first attempted human marrow infusions in 1956, treating patients with end-stage leukemia after high-dose chemotherapy and radiation.
The early results were almost uniformly fatal.
The patients died of infections, or of bleeding, or of what we would now recognize as graft-versus-host disease, although the concept hadn't been articulated yet.
The marrow either failed to engraft, or it engrafted and then attacked the recipient.
Or the recipient died of the chemo and radiation conditioning itself.
By the early 1960s, only a handful of patients had survived.
The field was, by any normal medical standard, a failure.
What kept it alive was a small number of people — Thomas chief among them — who believed the underlying biology had to be right.
The mice worked.
The principle was sound.
They just hadn't yet figured out the immunology.

The immunology turned out to be the key, and it came in the form of a discovery made in parallel by Jean Dausset in France, George Snell in Maine, and Baruj Benacerraf in New York: the existence of a set of cell-surface proteins called the human leukocyte antigens, or HLA, which serve as the body's "self" markers.

We'll spend the next segment on HLA in detail, but for now: every cell in your body carries HLA proteins on its surface, and your immune system is trained to recognize those specific HLA shapes as "self.
" Anyone else's HLA proteins, with different sequences, look like "non-self" — and the immune system attacks them.
This is the central reason organ transplants are rejected.
It is also the central reason bone marrow transplants either fail or, worse, cause graft-versus-host disease.

Once HLA was discovered and methods to type donors and recipients were developed, in the mid-1960s, Thomas — now at the Fred Hutchinson Cancer Research Center in Seattle, where he would spend the rest of his career — could finally do something his predecessors couldn't: match donors to recipients.
In 1968 and 1969, he and his Seattle team performed the first successful HLA-matched allogeneic bone marrow transplants.
Long-term survivors emerged.
Patients with previously incurable leukemias began to live for months, then years, then decades.
By the mid-1970s, the Seattle group could publish series of patients with acute leukemia in whom HSCT had produced durable, drug-free remissions.
The therapy worked, in the right patients, with the right donors.
Thomas built the Fred Hutchinson into the world center for transplantation.
His protocols — the conditioning regimens, the GVHD prophylaxis, the post-transplant care — became the global standard.
His trainees seeded transplant programs at academic centers around the world.

By the time he won the Nobel in 1990, more than ten thousand patients had been transplanted at his institution alone, and bone marrow transplantation was an established curative therapy for several leukemias, severe aplastic anemia, severe combined immunodeficiency, and a handful of genetic blood disorders.
Thomas himself was, by all accounts, a quiet and modest man, more comfortable at the bedside than on the lecture circuit.
He died in 2012, at age 92, having lived long enough to see the field he founded extend its reach into gene therapy, into pediatric cures for sickle cell, into the early CAR-T era.
He had built a procedure that, today, is performed on tens of thousands of patients every year worldwide, and that serves as the chassis for an entire generation of new cellular medicines.
There's a lesson in this history worth pausing on.
The first decade of human bone marrow transplants was a catastrophe by ordinary clinical standards.
Almost every patient died.
There were calls within the medical community to abandon the approach as obviously dangerous and unworkable.

The reason it didn't get abandoned is that a small number of people had a clear mechanistic understanding of why it should work and a stubborn belief that the missing piece — turned out to be HLA matching — was findable.
They were right.
But they had to wait twenty years to be vindicated.
This pattern, where a procedure looks like a failure until a key piece of biology gets discovered and changes everything, will repeat in the modern story of gene therapy and cellular medicine.
Keep it in mind.
The most transformative therapies often look like dead ends for years before they suddenly work.
